content:
# Acts Chapter 8 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 234-235 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_008_page_001.jpg, ACT_008_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Saul was consenting to his killing. And there arose on that day a great persecution against the church in Jerusalem, and they were all scattered throughout the regions of Judea and Samaria, except the apostles.

**2** And devout men carried Stephen to burial and made great lamentation over him.

**3** But Saul was ravaging the church, entering house after house, and dragging off both men and women, he delivered them to prison.

**4** Now those who had been scattered went about proclaiming the word.

**5** And Philip, having gone down to the city of Samaria, was preaching the Christ to them.

**6** And the crowds with one accord were giving heed to the things spoken by Philip, as they heard and saw the signs which he was doing.

**7** For unclean spirits, crying out with a loud voice, were coming out of many who had them, and many who were paralyzed and lame were healed.

**8** And there was great joy in that city.

**9** Now a certain man named Simon had previously been practicing sorcery in the city and astonishing the people of Samaria, claiming to be someone great.

**10** To whom they all were giving heed, from the least to the greatest, saying, "This one is the power of God which is called Great."

**11** And they were giving heed to him because for a considerable time he had been astonishing them with his sorceries.

**12** But when they believed Philip as he was proclaiming the good news concerning the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were being baptized, both men and women.

**13** And Simon himself also believed, and having been baptized, he was continuing steadfastly with Philip, and beholding signs and great works of power taking place, he was amazed.

**14** Now when the apostles in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent to them Peter and John,

**15** who, having come down, prayed for them that they might receive the Holy Spirit.

**16** For it had not yet fallen upon any of them; they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.

**17** Then they were laying their hands upon them, and they were receiving the Holy Spirit.

**18** Now when Simon saw that through the laying on of the hands of the apostles the Spirit was given, he offered them money,

**19** saying, "Give to me also this authority, so that upon whomever I lay my hands, he may receive the Holy Spirit."

**20** But Peter said to him, "May your silver perish with you, because you supposed you could obtain the gift of God through money.

**21** There is for you neither part nor share in this matter, for your heart is not right before God.

**22** Repent therefore of this wickedness of yours, and beseech the Lord, if perhaps the intention of your heart may be forgiven you.

**23** For I see that you are in the gall of bitterness and the bond of unrighteousness."

**24** And Simon answered and said, "Beseech the Lord on my behalf, so that nothing of what you have spoken may come upon me."

**25** They then, having solemnly testified and spoken the word of the Lord, were returning to Jerusalem, and were proclaiming the good news to many villages of the Samaritans.

**26** And an angel of the Lord spoke to Philip, saying, "Rise and go toward the south, upon the road that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza." This is a desert road.

**27** And he rose and went. And behold, a man, an Ethiopian eunuch, a court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was over all her treasure, who had come to Jerusalem to worship,

**28** and was returning and sitting in his chariot, and was reading the prophet Isaiah.

**29** And the Spirit said to Philip, "Approach and join yourself to this chariot."

**30** And Philip, having run up, heard him reading Isaiah the prophet, and said, "Do you then understand what you are reading?"

**31** And he said, "How could I, unless someone should guide me?" And he invited Philip, having come up, to sit with him.

**32** Now the passage of the Scripture which he was reading was this: "As a sheep he was led to the slaughter, and as a lamb before its shearer is silent, so he opens not his mouth.

**33** In his humiliation his judgment was taken away. His generation, who shall declare? For his life is taken from the earth."

**34** And the eunuch answered Philip and said, "I ask you, concerning whom does the prophet say this — concerning himself or concerning someone else?"

**35** And Philip, having opened his mouth and beginning from this Scripture, proclaimed to him the good news of Jesus.

**36** And as they were going along the road, they came upon some water, and the eunuch said, "Behold, water! What hinders me from being baptized?"

**37** [omitted in Sinaiticus]

**38** And he commanded the chariot to stand still, and they both went down into the water, both Philip and the eunuch, and he baptized him.

**39** And when they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord snatched Philip away, and the eunuch saw him no more, for he was going on his way rejoicing.

**40** But Philip was found at Azotus, and passing through, he was proclaiming the good news to all the cities until he came to Caesarea.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus, pages 234-235):**

The text is written in Sinaiticus's characteristic four-column scriptio continua format. Acts 7:55 concludes at the top of page 234, with Acts 8 beginning in the first column. The text continues across both pages, with Acts 9 beginning on page 235.

**Key Translation Decisions:**

1. **v.1 — "consenting" (suneudokon):** Rendered as "consenting" rather than "approving" to preserve the formal sense of active agreement. Sinaiticus reads ΣΥΝΕΥΔΟΚΩΝ, the present participle, indicating Saul's ongoing approval.

2. **v.3 — "ravaging" (elumaineto):** The imperfect middle ΕΛΥΜΑΙΝΕΤΟ conveys continuous, violent destruction — "ravaging" captures the force of this term more faithfully than "destroying."

3. **v.5 — "the city of Samaria":** Sinaiticus reads ΤΗΝ ΠΟΛΙΝ ΤΗΣ ΣΑΜΑΡΙΑΣ with the definite article, pointing to a specific city (likely Sebaste/Shechem) rather than the region generally.

4. **v.10 — "the power of God which is called Great":** Following the Sinaiticus reading Η ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ Η ΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝΗ ΜΕΓΑΛΗ. This is a significant theological claim — the Samaritans identified Simon with a divine emanation.

5. **v.13 — "works of power" (dunameis):** Rendered as "works of power" rather than "miracles" to preserve the connection to ΔΥΝΑΜΙΣ, maintaining the verbal echo with v.10 where Simon was called "the Power."

6. **v.16 — "fallen upon":** Preserving the spatial metaphor of ΕΠΙΠΕΠΤΩΚΟΣ (epipeptokos) — the Spirit "falls upon" rather than a more abstract rendering.

7. **v.20 — "May your silver perish with you":** Preserving Peter's direct, severe language. The optative ΕΙΗ ΕΙΣ ΑΠΩΛΕΙΑΝ carries the weight of a solemn imprecation.

8. **v.23 — "gall of bitterness and bond of unrighteousness":** Maintaining the Hebraic genitive constructions (ΧΟΛΗΝ ΠΙΚΡΙΑΣ, ΣΥΝΔΕΣΜΟΝ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ) rather than flattening to adjectives. These echo Deuteronomy 29:18 and Isaiah 58:6 (LXX).

9. **v.26 — "This is a desert road":** The narrator's explanatory gloss. Sinaiticus includes ΑΥΤΗ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΕΡΗΜΟΣ as a parenthetical note about the road, not about Gaza itself.

10. **v.37 — Omission:** Verse 37 (the eunuch's confession of faith) is absent from Codex Sinaiticus, consistent with its absence from the earliest and most reliable manuscripts. It is a later Western interpolation found in some minuscules and the margin of certain manuscripts.

11. **v.39 — "Spirit of the Lord snatched ... away":** ΠΝΕΥΜΑ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ΗΡΠΑΣΕΝ — Sinaiticus reads "Spirit of the Lord" rather than the variant "Holy Spirit fell upon the eunuch and an angel of the Lord snatched" found in some Western texts. The verb ΗΡΠΑΣΕΝ ("snatched away") conveys sudden, forceful removal.

12. **Consistent Terminology:** "Proclaiming the good news" (euangelizomenos) is maintained throughout (vv. 4, 12, 25, 35, 40) rather than alternating with "preaching the gospel," preserving the verbal unity of the Greek ΕΥΑΓΓΕΛΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΣ. "Preaching" (kerusson) is reserved for ΚΗΡΥΣΣΩΝ (v. 5).

— LOGOS
